name: Anubis (by Nephthys)
shortDescription: Anubis is the ancient Egyptian god of embalming and the dead, guiding souls into the afterlife. He is typically depicted with the head of a jackal.
description: Anubis, in ancient Egyptian religion, is the jackal-headed god associated with mummification and the afterlife. His primary role was to oversee the embalming process and conduct the souls of the dead into the afterlife. According to some myths, Anubis was the illegitimate son of Nephthys and Osiris, conceived in secret and later adopted by Isis. This origin story is not universally accepted, with other accounts stating his parentage differently. Anubis played a crucial role in the 'Weighing of the Heart' ceremony, where the deceased's heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). If the heart was lighter than the feather, the soul was worthy and could proceed to the afterlife; if heavier, it was devoured by Ammit. Anubis's association with jackals likely stems from the animal's tendency to scavenge in cemeteries. He was revered as a protector of graves and a guardian of the dead, ensuring their safe passage into the next world. His importance diminished somewhat with the rise of Osiris as the primary god of the afterlife, but he remained a vital figure in funerary rites and beliefs. He is depicted as a jackal or a man with a jackal's head, often holding the ankh, the symbol of life. His presence was essential in ensuring the proper transition of the deceased from the earthly realm to the eternal one.
epithets: He Who is Upon His Mountain

sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.author: Various Scribes

sources.title: Myths and Symbols of Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Lucia Gahlin

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to Anubis
worshipPractices.description: Ancient Egyptians offered food, drink, and various goods to Anubis at his temples and shrines. These offerings were intended to appease the god and ensure safe passage into the afterlife for the deceased. Priests dedicated to Anubis also conducted rituals and prayers on behalf of the living and the dead.

worshipPractices.name: Funerary Rites
worshipPractices.description: Anubis played a crucial role in funerary rites, overseeing the embalming process and the weighing of the heart ceremony. Embalmers wore Anubis masks during mummification to embody the god and ensure the proper preservation of the body. The weighing of the heart determined the deceased's worthiness to enter the afterlife.

festivals.name: Festival of Khoiak
festivals.timing: Throughout the month of Khoiak (roughly October/November)
festivals.description: While not solely dedicated to Anubis, the Festival of Khoiak included rituals and ceremonies honoring various deities associated with death and rebirth, including Anubis. Mummification rites and agricultural celebrations were common, symbolizing renewal and the cycle of life and death.

cultural.modernLegacy: Anubis remains a recognizable and popular figure in modern culture, often depicted in art, literature, and films related to ancient Egypt. He frequently represents themes of death, the afterlife, and the mysteries of ancient civilizations, often appearing in video games and fantasy novels.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Hermes Psychopompos, like Anubis, guides the souls of the dead to the afterlife. Both deities act as conductors between the world of the living and the realm of the dead.
Charun, an Etruscan deity, is depicted as a psychopomp who escorts the deceased to the underworld. Similar to Anubis, he's associated with death and the transition to the afterlife.

Xolotl, the Aztec god of lightning and death, also guides souls and guards the sun as it passes through the underworld each night. He shares the role of psychopomp and is associated with the journey through death, similar to Anubis.

The Ankou of Celtic folklore is a figure associated with death who collects the souls of the dead. While not a guide, he is a direct representation of death and its inevitability, a role Anubis's association with mummification reflects.

associations.name: Jackal
associations.significance: Anubis is depicted with the head of a jackal, an animal associated with cemeteries due to its scavenging habits, thus linking him to death and protection of the deceased.

associations.name: Black
associations.significance: Black symbolizes the fertile soil of the Nile, representing regeneration and rebirth, and is often associated with Anubis in relation to mummification and the afterlife.

associations.name: Guidance
associations.significance: As the conductor of souls, Anubis provides guidance and protection to the deceased on their journey through the underworld, ensuring their safe passage.

associations.name: Secrecy
associations.significance: Born of Nephthys's hidden union, Anubis's parentage represents hidden knowledge and the mysteries surrounding death and the afterlife, adding to his enigmatic nature.

associations.name: Sand
associations.significance: Sand represents the desert landscapes where cemeteries were often located, connecting Anubis to the physical space of death and burial.

associations.name: Sirius
associations.significance: Sirius was associated with new beginnings and the flooding of the Nile, linking to rebirth, which ties into Anubis's role in the afterlife and mummification.

associations.name: Myrrh
associations.significance: Myrrh was used in the embalming process, linking it to Anubis's role in mummification and preserving the body for the afterlife.

companions.name: Osiris
companions.description: Osiris is Anubis' adoptive father. Osiris played a crucial role in legitimizing Anubis and giving him a place within the divine pantheon.

companions.name: Nephthys
companions.description: Nephthys is Anubis' mother. She secretly bore him and sought to protect him from Set's wrath.

companions.name: Thoth
companions.description: Thoth often assists Anubis in the weighing of the heart ceremony. He records the results to determine the fate of the deceased's soul.

companions.name: Ammut
companions.description: Ammut, the Devourer of the Dead, stands beside Anubis during the weighing of the heart. If the heart is heavier than the feather of Ma'at, Ammut devours the soul, preventing it from entering the afterlife.

companions.name: Embalmers
companions.description: The embalmers, priests dedicated to the mummification process, are essentially Anubis' earthly servants. They perform the rituals and tasks necessary to prepare the dead for the afterlife under his guidance.
